Since C2 is attached to a methyl group (C1) and C3 is attached to a methyl group (C4), every carbon atom in the newly formed benzene ring will have a methyl substituent. This results in the formation of hexamethylbenzene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the correct name for the given compound:
options: phenylketone
benzaldehyde
benzylcarbonyl
phenylaldehyde
questions.solution:
The given structure consists of a benzene ring attached directly to a formyl group ($-CHO$).

The $-CHO$ functional group represents an aldehyde.

When an aldehyde group is attached directly to a benzene ring, the compound is named benzaldehyde.

Therefore, the correct name for the given compound is benzaldehyde.

Answer: benzaldehyde

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
Step 1: Reductive ozonolysis of 1-methylcyclopentene cleaves the $\text{C=C}$ double bond to form a dicarbonyl compound. The methyl-substituted carbon becomes a ketone, and the unsubstituted carbon becomes an aldehyde.
The intermediate product 'A' is 5-oxohexanal: $\text{CH}_3-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHO}$.

Step 2: Treatment of 'A' with Toluene-4-sulphonic acid (a strong acid) and heat ($\Delta$) initiates an acid-catalyzed intramolecular aldol condensation.
Enolization occurs at the terminal methyl group, and the resulting nucleophilic enol carbon attacks the electrophilic aldehyde carbonyl carbon to form a stable 6-membered ring.
This cyclization yields a $\beta$-hydroxy ketone intermediate, 3-hydroxycyclohexanone.

Step 3: Under the acidic conditions with heating, the $\beta$-hydroxy ketone undergoes dehydration (elimination of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$) to form the thermodynamically more stable, conjugated $\alpha,\beta$-unsaturated ketone.
The final major product is cyclohex-2-en-1-one, which corresponds to the structure in option (A).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:

$+$
questions.solution:
The reaction between benzaldehyde and acetone in the presence of a base and heat is a crossed aldol condensation (Claisen-Schmidt condensation).

Acetone has $\alpha$-hydrogens, so it forms an enolate ion in the presence of $OH^-$.

$CH_3-CO-CH_3 + OH^- \rightleftharpoons CH_3-CO-CH_2^- + H_2O$

Benzaldehyde lacks $\alpha$-hydrogens and acts as the electrophile. The enolate attacks the carbonyl carbon of benzaldehyde.

$C_6H_5-CHO + CH_3-CO-CH_2^- \rightarrow C_6H_5-CH(O^-)-CH_2-CO-CH_3$

Protonation yields the $\beta$-hydroxy ketone.

$C_6H_5-CH(O^-)-CH_2-CO-CH_3 + H_2O \rightarrow C_6H_5-CH(OH)-CH_2-CO-CH_3 + OH^-$

Upon heating ($\Delta$), the $\beta$-hydroxy ketone undergoes dehydration to form a stable $\alpha,\beta$-unsaturated ketone, which is highly conjugated with the benzene ring.

$C_6H_5-CH(OH)-CH_2-CO-CH_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} C_6H_5-CH=CH-CO-CH_3 + H_2O$

The major product is benzylideneacetone, which corresponds to the structure in the first option.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product $\mathrm{T}$ formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is a crossed aldol condensation (specifically, Claisen-Schmidt condensation) between benzaldehyde and acetone in the presence of a base ($OH^-$).

Benzaldehyde lacks $\alpha$-hydrogens, so it cannot form an enolate ion. Acetone has $\alpha$-hydrogens and forms an enolate ion in the presence of the base.

The base abstracts an $\alpha$-proton from acetone to form the enolate:
$CH_3COCH_3 + OH^- \rightleftharpoons CH_3COCH_2^- + H_2O$

The nucleophilic enolate ion then attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of benzaldehyde:
$C_6H_5CHO + CH_3COCH_2^- \rightarrow C_6H_5CH(O^-)CH_2COCH_3$

Protonation from water yields the $\beta$-hydroxy ketone (aldol):
$C_6H_5CH(O^-)CH_2COCH_3 + H_2O \rightarrow C_6H_5CH(OH)CH_2COCH_3 + OH^-$

This intermediate readily undergoes dehydration (loss of a water molecule) because the resulting double bond is highly conjugated with both the benzene ring and the carbonyl group, providing extra stability. This leads to the formation of an $\alpha,\beta$-unsaturated ketone:
$C_6H_5CH(OH)CH_2COCH_3 \xrightarrow{-H_2O} C_6H_5CH=CHCOCH_3$

The final major product T is benzylideneacetone (4-phenylbut-3-en-2-one), which corresponds to the structure in Option (B).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reagents is traditionally used to test a patient's urine for the detection of diabetes?
options: Fenton's reagent
Benedict's reagent
Nessler's reagent
Tollens' reagent
questions.solution:
Diabetes is characterized by the presence of excess glucose in the urine, a condition known as glycosuria.

Glucose is a reducing sugar because it contains a free aldehyde group in its open-chain form.

Benedict's reagent, which contains copper(II) sulfate, sodium citrate, and sodium carbonate, is traditionally used to test for the presence of reducing sugars in clinical samples like urine.

When heated with a reducing sugar, the blue copper(II) ions are reduced to a red precipitate of copper(I) oxide ($\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$).

Fenton's reagent is a solution of hydrogen peroxide and an iron catalyst used to oxidize contaminants.

Nessler's reagent is used to detect the presence of ammonia.

Tollens' reagent is used to distinguish between aldehydes and ketones but is not the traditional clinical test for urine glucose.

Answer: Benedict's reagent

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the Kiliani-Fischer synthesis for the conversion of an aldose into its next higher homologue, the reagent employed in the first step is:
options: $\mathrm{NH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$
$\mathrm{HCN}$
$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NHNH}_{2}$
$\mathrm{Br}_{2} / \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
questions.solution:
The Kiliani-Fischer synthesis is a method used to increase the carbon chain length of an aldose by one carbon atom.

In the first step of this synthesis, the aldose is treated with hydrogen cyanide ($\mathrm{HCN}$). The nucleophilic addition of cyanide ion to the carbonyl group of the aldose forms a mixture of two epimeric cyanohydrins.

These cyanohydrins are subsequently hydrolyzed to aldonic acids (or their lactones) and then reduced to yield the higher homologous aldoses.

Therefore, the reagent employed in the first step is $\mathrm{HCN}$.

Answer: $\mathrm{HCN}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Select the correct order of the given compounds with respect to their increasing rate of reaction with $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$.
options: < <
<
< <
<
questions.solution:
The reaction of carbonyl compounds with $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ involves the nucleophilic addition of a hydride ion ($\mathrm{H}^{-}$) to the electrophilic carbonyl carbon.

The rate of nucleophilic addition depends on two primary factors:
1. Steric hindrance: Bulky alkyl groups around the carbonyl carbon block the approach of the nucleophile, decreasing the reaction rate.
2. Electronic factors (+I effect): Alkyl groups are electron-donating. More alkyl groups or larger alkyl groups increase the electron density on the carbonyl carbon, reducing its electrophilicity and thus decreasing the reaction rate.

Comparing the given compounds:
- Pentanal is an aldehyde. It has only one alkyl group attached to the carbonyl carbon, resulting in the least steric hindrance and the minimum +I effect. Hence, it is the most reactive.
- Pentan-3-one is a ketone with two ethyl groups attached to the carbonyl carbon. It has more steric hindrance and a greater +I effect than pentanal, making it less reactive.
- 2,4-dimethylpentan-3-one is a ketone with two bulky isopropyl groups. It has the maximum steric hindrance and the strongest +I effect among the three, making it the least reactive.

Therefore, the increasing order of reactivity towards $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ is:
2,4-dimethylpentan-3-one < pentan-3-one < pentanal.

Answer: < <

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the appropriate reagent to carry out the following transformation:
options: $\mathrm{OsO}_{4}$
$\mathrm{PhCO}_{3} \mathrm{H}$
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$
$\mathrm{PhCO}_{2} \mathrm{H}$
questions.solution:
The given transformation involves the conversion of a cyclic ketone (cyclopentanone) into a cyclic ester or lactone.

This reaction is known as the Baeyer-Villiger oxidation, which inserts an oxygen atom adjacent to the carbonyl group.

The Baeyer-Villiger oxidation is typically carried out using a peroxyacid (peracid) such as peroxybenzoic acid ($\mathrm{PhCO}_{3} \mathrm{H}$), meta-chloroperoxybenzoic acid (m-CPBA), or peracetic acid ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CO}_{3}\mathrm{H}$).

Among the given options, $\mathrm{PhCO}_{3} \mathrm{H}$ is a peroxyacid and is the appropriate reagent for this transformation.

$\mathrm{OsO}_{4}$ is used for the dihydroxylation of alkenes.

$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ is a strong oxidizing agent used for oxidizing alcohols.

$\mathrm{PhCO}_{2} \mathrm{H}$ is a carboxylic acid and does not act as an oxidizing agent for this reaction.

Answer: $\mathrm{PhCO}_{3} \mathrm{H}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When glucose is treated with an excess of $\mathrm{HIO}_{4}$, the products formed are:
options: six moles of HCOOH
five moles of HCOOH and one mole of HCHO
one mole of oxalic acid $(\mathrm{COOHCOOH})$, three moles of HCOOH and one mole of HCHO
one mole of glyoxal $(\mathrm{CHOCHO})$, one mole of HCHO and three moles of HCOOH
questions.solution:
Glucose is an aldohexose with the open-chain structure $\mathrm{CHO-(CHOH)_{4}-CH_{2}OH}$.

Periodic acid ($\mathrm{HIO}_{4}$) cleaves the C-C bonds of vicinal diols, $\alpha$-hydroxy aldehydes, and $\alpha$-hydroxy ketones. During this oxidative cleavage:
1. A primary alcohol ($\mathrm{-CH_{2}OH}$) is oxidized to formaldehyde ($\mathrm{HCHO}$).
2. A secondary alcohol ($\mathrm{-CHOH-}$) flanked by cleavable groups is oxidized to formic acid ($\mathrm{HCOOH}$).
3. An aldehyde ($\mathrm{-CHO}$) attached to a cleavable group is oxidized to formic acid ($\mathrm{HCOOH}$).

In one molecule of glucose, there are five cleavable C-C bonds:
The C1 aldehyde group is oxidized to $1$ mole of $\mathrm{HCOOH}$.
The four secondary alcohol groups (C2, C3, C4, C5) are each oxidized to yield $4$ moles of $\mathrm{HCOOH}$.
The C6 primary alcohol group is oxidized to $1$ mole of $\mathrm{HCHO}$.

Thus, the oxidation of one mole of glucose with excess $\mathrm{HIO}_{4}$ yields a total of five moles of $\mathrm{HCOOH}$ and one mole of $\mathrm{HCHO}$.

Answer: five moles of HCOOH and one mole of HCHO

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence:

$\xrightarrow[\text{(ii) NaOH, } \Delta]{\text{(i) 4 eq. NaOMe, } \Delta}$ Product
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the Weiss-Cook reaction.

The first reactant is dimethyl 3-oxopentanedioate (dimethyl acetonedicarboxylate). The presence of the coefficient 2 indicates that 2 equivalents of this diester react with 1 equivalent of glyoxal.

In the first step, 4 equivalents of NaOMe are used to deprotonate the 4 active methylene groups (two from each equivalent of the diester). The enolates undergo a double condensation with the two aldehyde groups of glyoxal, followed by cyclization. This forms the bicyclic intermediate: tetramethyl bicyclo[3.3.0]octane-3,7-dione-2,4,6,8-tetracarboxylate.

In the second step, the intermediate is treated with aqueous NaOH and heat ($\Delta$). This leads to the saponification of all four ester groups, converting them into carboxylate salts. Upon implicit acidic workup, the corresponding tetracarboxylic acid is formed.

Since all four $-COOH$ groups in the resulting acid are in the $\beta$-position with respect to the ketone carbonyls (at C3 and C7), the molecule is a $\beta$-keto acid. $\beta$-keto acids readily undergo complete decarboxylation upon heating ($\Delta$).

Therefore, all four carboxylic acid groups are lost as $CO_2$, yielding bicyclo[3.3.0]octane-3,7-dione as the final major product.

This structure corresponds to the first option.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is a Robinson annulation, which involves a Michael addition followed by an intramolecular aldol condensation.

The base abstracts the acidic $\alpha$-proton from isobutyraldehyde to form an enolate ion.

$(CH_3)_2CH-CHO + CH_3O^{-} \rightarrow (CH_3)_2C^{-}-CHO + CH_3OH$

The enolate acts as a Michael donor and attacks the $\beta$-carbon of methyl vinyl ketone.

$(CH_3)_2C^{-}-CHO + CH_2=CH-CO-CH_3 \rightarrow OHC-C(CH_3)_2-CH_2-CH_2-CO-CH_3$

This intermediate is 2,2-dimethyl-5-oxohexanal. An intramolecular aldol condensation then occurs. The base abstracts an $\alpha$-proton from the terminal methyl group of the ketone to form a new enolate, which nucleophilically attacks the aldehyde carbonyl carbon to form a stable six-membered ring.

$OHC-C(CH_3)_2-CH_2-CH_2-CO-CH_2^{-} \rightarrow$ 5-hydroxy-4,4-dimethylcyclohexan-1-one

Upon heating, dehydration takes place. The hydroxyl group is eliminated along with a proton from the adjacent methylene group, as the other adjacent carbon is quaternary and lacks protons. This elimination yields a double bond in conjugation with the ketone carbonyl.

The final major product is 4,4-dimethylcyclohex-2-en-1-one.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Predict the major product of the following aldol condensation reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 4-methylpentanal, which has the structure $\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHO}$.

In the presence of a base ($\text{OH}^-$), an aldol condensation takes place. The base removes an acidic $\alpha$-proton from one molecule of 4-methylpentanal to form an enolate ion:
$\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHO} \xrightarrow{\text{OH}^-} \text{CH}_3-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}^{-}-\text{CHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$

This enolate acts as a nucleophile and attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of a second molecule of 4-methylpentanal:
$\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHO} + \text{CH}_3-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}^{-}-\text{CHO} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{O}^-)-\text{CH}(\text{CHO})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)_2$

Protonation from water yields the aldol ($\beta$-hydroxy aldehyde):
$\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{OH})-\text{CH}(\text{CHO})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)_2$

The reaction conditions specify dehydration ($-\text{H}_2\text{O}$). The aldol undergoes elimination of water (loss of the $\beta$-hydroxyl group and an $\alpha$-proton) to form an $\alpha,\beta$-unsaturated aldehyde:
$\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}=\text{C}(\text{CHO})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)_2$

Analyzing the final product, the double bond is substituted with an isopentyl group ($\text{-CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)_2$) on the $\beta$-carbon and an isobutyl group ($\text{-CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)_2$) on the $\alpha$-carbon (along with the aldehyde group).

Comparing this structure with the given options, Option (A) perfectly matches this connectivity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the most suitable reagents for the following transformation:
options: $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{NEt}$ and $\mathrm{NaOH}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{NEt}$ and $\mathrm{NaOCH}_{3}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{NEt}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{H}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{NEt}$ and $\mathrm{HCl}$
questions.solution:
The given transformation is the conversion of a ketone (cyclopentanone) into an imine ($N$-ethylcyclopentanimine).

This reaction involves the nucleophilic addition of a primary amine, ethylamine ($\mathrm{H_2NEt}$), to the carbonyl group, followed by the elimination of a water molecule.

The formation of an imine is an acid-catalyzed process. The acid serves two purposes:
1. It protonates the carbonyl oxygen, increasing the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon.
2. It protonates the hydroxyl group in the carbinolamine intermediate, converting it into a better leaving group ($\mathrm{H_2O}$) for the dehydration step.

However, the choice of acid is crucial. If a strong acid like $\mathrm{HCl}$ is used, the basic amine will be completely protonated to form an ammonium salt ($\mathrm{EtNH_3^+}$), rendering it non-nucleophilic and halting the reaction. If the medium is basic (using $\mathrm{NaOH}$ or $\mathrm{NaOCH_3}$), there is no acid to catalyze the dehydration step.

Therefore, mildly acidic conditions (pH $\approx 4-5$) are optimal for this reaction. A weak acid like acetic acid ($\mathrm{CH_3CO_2H}$) provides the necessary protons without completely protonating the nucleophilic amine. Thus, the most suitable reagents are $\mathrm{H_2NEt}$ and $\mathrm{CH_3CO_2H}$.

Answer: $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{NEt}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{H}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the major product obtained when cyclohexanone is treated with an aqueous $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3}$ solution?
questions.solution:
Cyclohexanone contains $\alpha$-hydrogens. When treated with a mild base like aqueous $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3}$, it undergoes an aldol addition reaction.

The base abstracts an $\alpha$-hydrogen from one molecule of cyclohexanone to form a nucleophilic enolate ion.

This enolate ion then attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of a second cyclohexanone molecule.

Subsequent protonation from water yields a $\beta$-hydroxy ketone. Specifically, the $\alpha$-carbon of the first cyclohexanone ring becomes bonded to the former carbonyl carbon of the second ring, which is now converted into a hydroxyl group.

The resulting product is 2-(1-hydroxycyclohexyl)cyclohexan-1-one, which matches the structure given in Option (C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reagents reacts in three distinct ways with formaldehyde ($\mathrm{HCHO}$), acetaldehyde ($\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CHO}$), and benzaldehyde ($\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_5\mathrm{CHO}$) to yield different types of products?
options: Ammonia
Phenylhydrazine
Hydrogen chloride
Fehling's solution
questions.solution:
Formaldehyde reacts with ammonia to form urotropine (hexamethylenetetramine), which has a cage-like structure.

$6\mathrm{HCHO} + 4\mathrm{NH}_3 \rightarrow (\mathrm{CH}_2)_6\mathrm{N}_4 + 6\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$

Acetaldehyde reacts with ammonia to form an addition product, acetaldehyde ammonia, which loses water to form an imine.

$\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CHO} + \mathrm{NH}_3 \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH})\mathrm{NH}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{NH} + \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$

Benzaldehyde reacts with ammonia to form a complex di-imine known as hydrobenzamide.

$3\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_5\mathrm{CHO} + 2\mathrm{NH}_3 \rightarrow (\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_5\mathrm{CH})_3\mathrm{N}_2 + 3\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$

Phenylhydrazine reacts with all three aldehydes to form their respective phenylhydrazones, which is the same type of reaction.

Fehling's solution is reduced by formaldehyde and acetaldehyde but not by benzaldehyde, giving only two distinct behaviors.

Answer: Ammonia

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is cytidine, a nucleoside consisting of a cytosine base attached to a ribose sugar ring.

The ribose moiety contains a vicinal diol (1,2-diol) system at the C2' and C3' positions.

Periodic acid ($HIO_4$) is a specific oxidizing agent that cleaves the carbon-carbon bond of vicinal diols, converting the hydroxyl-bearing carbons into carbonyl groups (aldehydes or ketones).

In this reaction, the C2'-C3' bond of the ribose ring undergoes oxidative cleavage:
1. The C2' carbon is oxidized to an aldehyde group (-CHO).
2. The C3' carbon is also oxidized to an aldehyde group (-CHO).
3. The primary alcohol at the C5' position (-CH_2OH) is not part of a vicinal diol and thus remains unaffected by $HIO_4$.

As a result, the five-membered sugar ring opens to form a dialdehyde. The C1' carbon remains bonded to the cytosine base, the ether oxygen, and the new C2' formyl group. The C4' carbon remains bonded to the -CH_2OH group, the ether oxygen, and the new C3' formyl group.

This structure exactly matches the product shown in Option (B).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Consider the given reaction sequence. Identify the structure of the major product $\mathrm{Z}$.
questions.solution:
The reaction sequence involves the following steps:

1. Protection: The starting material, 5-hydroxy-2-pentanone, reacts with 1,3-propanediol in the presence of an acid catalyst ($\text{H}^+$). This selectively protects the more reactive ketone group as a cyclic ketal (1,3-dioxane derivative), while the primary alcohol remains unaffected.

2. Oxidation: Pyridinium chlorochromate (PCC) oxidizes the primary alcohol group to an aldehyde. The ketal group is stable under these mildly acidic, non-aqueous conditions.

3. Wittig Reaction: The newly formed aldehyde reacts with the Wittig reagent, $(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5)_3\text{P}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$. This converts the aldehyde group ($-\text{CHO}$) into an alkene ($-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$), extending the carbon chain by four carbons.

4. Deprotection: Treatment with aqueous acid ($\text{H}^+$) hydrolyzes the cyclic ketal back to the original ketone group.

The final major product $\text{Z}$ is a straight-chain compound with a ketone at the C2 position and a double bond between the C5 and C6 positions (non-5-en-2-one). This corresponds to the structure shown in option (B).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
In which of the following reactions is a new carbon-carbon bond formed?
(i) Aldol condensation
(ii) Kolbe's reaction
(iii) Reimer-Tiemann reaction
(iv) Wurtz-Fittig reaction
options: (i) and (iii) only
(ii) and (iii) only
(i), (iii) and (iv) only
(i), (ii), (iii) and (iv)
questions.solution:
In Aldol condensation, an enolate ion attacks a carbonyl carbon, forming a new carbon-carbon bond between two aldehyde or ketone molecules.

In Kolbe's reaction, sodium phenoxide reacts with $CO_2$ to form salicylic acid. A new carbon-carbon bond is formed between the aromatic ring and the carbon atom of $CO_2$.

In the Reimer-Tiemann reaction, phenol reacts with chloroform in the presence of a base to form salicylaldehyde. A new carbon-carbon bond is formed between the aromatic ring and the carbon atom from chloroform (via a dichlorocarbene intermediate).

In the Wurtz-Fittig reaction, an aryl halide and an alkyl halide react with sodium metal to form an alkylarene. A new carbon-carbon bond is formed between the aryl carbon and the alkyl carbon.

Therefore, a new carbon-carbon bond is formed in all four reactions.

Answer: (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds can be oxidized by periodic acid ($\mathrm{HIO}_4$)?
options: All of these
questions.solution:
Periodic acid ($\mathrm{HIO}_4$) is an oxidizing agent that cleaves carbon-carbon bonds in compounds containing adjacent (vicinal) functional groups such as diols, $\alpha$-hydroxy ketones, $\alpha$-diketones, $\alpha$-hydroxy aldehydes, and $\alpha$-amino alcohols.

Let us analyze the given compounds:
1. Hexane-3,4-diol: It contains two adjacent hydroxyl groups ($-OH$) on carbons 3 and 4. This is a vicinal diol, which undergoes oxidative cleavage by $\mathrm{HIO}_4$ to form two molecules of propanal.
2. 4-hydroxyhexan-3-one: It contains a hydroxyl group on carbon 4 adjacent to a ketone group on carbon 3. This is an $\alpha$-hydroxy ketone, which is cleaved by $\mathrm{HIO}_4$ to form propanal and propanoic acid.
3. Hexane-3,4-dione: It contains two adjacent ketone groups on carbons 3 and 4. This is an $\alpha$-diketone, which is cleaved by $\mathrm{HIO}_4$ to form two molecules of propanoic acid.

Since all three compounds possess the required adjacent functional groups, they can all be oxidized by periodic acid.

Answer: All of these

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When dissolved in a dilute aqueous solution, formaldehyde predominantly exists as:
options: Formaldehyde
Methylene glycol
Paraldehyde
Trioxymethylene
questions.solution:
Formaldehyde ($\text{HCHO}$) reacts with water in a dilute aqueous solution to undergo hydration.

The reaction is:
$\text{HCHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_2(\text{OH})_2$

The product $\text{CH}_2(\text{OH})_2$ is a gem-diol known as methylene glycol.

Due to the absence of steric hindrance and the high electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon in formaldehyde, the equilibrium lies almost completely to the right. Thus, in a dilute aqueous solution, formaldehyde predominantly exists as methylene glycol (nearly $99.9\%$).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following carbonyl compounds is the most stable, and which one is the least stable?
(i) $\mathrm{HCHO}$
(ii) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CHO}$
(iii) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$
(iv) $\mathrm{Cl}_{3}\mathrm{CCHO}$
options: All four compounds are equally stable
(iii) is the most stable and (i) is the least stable
(iii) is the most stable and (iv) is the least stable
(iv) is the most stable and (i) is the least stable
questions.solution:
The stability of a carbonyl compound depends on the nature of the groups attached to the carbonyl carbon. The carbonyl carbon possesses a partial positive charge due to the higher electronegativity of the oxygen atom.

Electron-donating groups, such as alkyl groups, stabilize the partial positive charge on the carbonyl carbon through the +I (inductive) effect and hyperconjugation. Conversely, electron-withdrawing groups destabilize the carbonyl carbon by intensifying the positive charge through the -I effect.

In $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$ (iii), there are two electron-donating methyl groups attached to the carbonyl carbon. This provides the maximum +I effect and hyperconjugation among the given compounds, making it the most stable.

In $\mathrm{Cl}_{3}\mathrm{CCHO}$ (iv), the $-\mathrm{CCl}_{3}$ group exerts a strong -I effect due to the presence of three highly electronegative chlorine atoms. This strongly destabilizes the carbonyl group, making it the least stable.

Therefore, (iii) is the most stable and (iv) is the least stable.

Answer: (iii) is the most stable and (iv) is the least stable

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following sequence of reactions:
questions.solution:
The given starting material is acetophenone ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COCH}_3$).

Step (i): Acetophenone is treated with a strong oxidizing agent, acidified sodium dichromate ($\text{Na}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7 / \text{H}^+$) under heating. Methyl ketones undergo oxidative cleavage under these vigorous conditions to form carboxylic acids. The methyl group is oxidized and lost as $\text{CO}_2$, yielding benzoic acid ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOH}$).
$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COCH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{Na}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7, \text{H}^+, \Delta} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOH}$

Step (ii): The resulting benzoic acid is then reacted with ethanol ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$) in the presence of an acid catalyst ($\text{H}^+$). This is a standard Fischer esterification reaction. The continuous removal of water drives the equilibrium forward to form the ester, ethyl benzoate.
$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOH} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+, -\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOCH}_2\text{CH}_3$

The final major product is ethyl benzoate, which corresponds to the structure in option (D).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the compound shown below:


Which of the following statements is correct regarding this compound?
options: It neither forms a dioxime nor gives a positive iodoform test.
It both forms a dioxime and gives a positive iodoform test.
It forms a dioxime.
It gives a positive iodoform test.
questions.solution:
The given compound is ethyl acetoacetate, which contains a ketone group and an ester group.

Reaction with hydroxylamine ($\text{NH}_2\text{OH}$):
Hydroxylamine reacts with aldehydes and ketones to form oximes, but it does not react with esters under normal conditions. Since the compound has only one ketone group, it forms a mono-oxime, not a dioxime.

Iodoform test ($\text{I}_2/\text{NaOH}$):
Although the compound contains a $\text{CH}_3\text{CO}-$ group, it also has an active methylene ($-\text{CH}_2-$) group flanked by two carbonyls. The protons of the active methylene group are more acidic than the methyl protons. Therefore, iodination preferentially occurs at the $-\text{CH}_2-$ group to form $\text{CH}_3\text{COCI}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5$.

Subsequent nucleophilic attack by the base ($\text{OH}^-$) on the more electrophilic ketone carbonyl leads to the cleavage of the C-C bond, producing acetate ion ($\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-$) and ethyl diiodoacetate ($\text{CHI}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5$).

Since iodoform ($\text{CHI}_3$) is not produced, the compound does not give a positive iodoform test.

Thus, the compound neither forms a dioxime nor gives a positive iodoform test.

Answer: It neither forms a dioxime nor gives a positive iodoform test.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following carbonyl compounds: $\mathrm{HCHO}$, $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CHO}$, and $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$.
Which of these compounds have the highest and the lowest hydration equilibrium constants, respectively?
options: Highest: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$, Lowest: $\mathrm{HCHO}$
Highest: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CHO}$, Lowest: $\mathrm{HCHO}$
Highest: $\mathrm{HCHO}$, Lowest: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CHO}$
Highest: $\mathrm{HCHO}$, Lowest: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$
questions.solution:
The hydration of a carbonyl compound involves the nucleophilic addition of water to the carbonyl carbon to form a gem-diol.

The equilibrium constant for this reaction depends on the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon and the steric hindrance around it.

Alkyl groups exert an electron-donating inductive effect (+I effect), which decreases the positive charge on the carbonyl carbon, thereby reducing its electrophilicity. Alkyl groups also increase steric hindrance, making the nucleophilic attack more difficult and destabilizing the tetrahedral intermediate.

Formaldehyde ($\mathrm{HCHO}$) has no alkyl groups, making its carbonyl carbon the most electrophilic and least sterically hindered. Thus, it has the highest hydration equilibrium constant.

Acetone ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$) has two methyl groups, providing the maximum +I effect and steric hindrance among the given compounds. Thus, it has the lowest hydration equilibrium constant.

Acetaldehyde ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CHO}$) has one methyl group, placing it intermediate between formaldehyde and acetone.

Therefore, the highest is $\mathrm{HCHO}$ and the lowest is $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$.

Answer: Highest: $\mathrm{HCHO}$, Lowest: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Propanal and propanone are isomers with the same molecular formula $\left(\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{O}\right)$. Which of the following statements correctly compares their boiling points?
options: Both have the same boiling point.
The boiling point of propanal is lower than that of propanone.
Their boiling points cannot be compared based on the given information.
The boiling point of propanal is higher than that of propanone.
questions.solution:
Propanal ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CHO}$) and propanone ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$) are functional isomers with the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{O}$.

The boiling point of a compound depends on the strength of its intermolecular forces. Both propanal and propanone have dipole-dipole interactions due to the polar carbonyl group.

In propanone, the carbonyl carbon is attached to two electron-donating methyl groups, which increases the polarity of the $\mathrm{C=O}$ bond compared to propanal, where the carbonyl carbon is attached to only one ethyl group. As a result, propanone has a higher dipole moment than propanal.

Due to the higher dipole moment, the dipole-dipole interactions are stronger in propanone than in propanal. Consequently, more energy is required to overcome these forces in propanone, leading to a higher boiling point for propanone ($\approx 329 \text{ K}$) compared to propanal ($\approx 322 \text{ K}$).

Thus, the boiling point of propanal is lower than that of propanone.

Answer: The boiling point of propanal is lower than that of propanone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The reaction shown below is an example of:
options: Intramolecular Cannizzaro reaction
Intermolecular Cannizzaro reaction
Tischenko reaction
Crossed Cannizzaro reaction
questions.solution:
The given reactant is glyoxylic acid ($\text{CHO-COOH}$), which is an aldehyde lacking $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms.

When treated with a base ($\text{OH}^-$), aldehydes without $\alpha$-hydrogens undergo a disproportionation reaction known as the Cannizzaro reaction. In this process, one molecule of the aldehyde is oxidized to a carboxylic acid (or carboxylate ion in basic medium), and another molecule is reduced to an alcohol.

In the given reaction:
1. One molecule of glyoxylic acid is oxidized to form the oxalate ion ($\text{COO}^-\text{-COO}^-$).
2. The second molecule of glyoxylic acid is reduced to form the glycolate ion ($\text{CH}_2\text{OH-COO}^-$).

Since the reaction involves two separate molecules of the same aldehyde reacting with each other, it is an example of an intermolecular Cannizzaro reaction.

Answer: Intermolecular Cannizzaro reaction

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Fehling's solution can be used to distinguish between which of the following pairs of compounds?
options: None of the above pairs
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CHO}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$
Both of the above pairs
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CHO}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CHO}$
questions.solution:
Fehling's solution is a mild oxidizing agent used to differentiate aliphatic aldehydes from aromatic aldehydes and ketones.

Aliphatic aldehydes like $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CHO}$ reduce Fehling's solution to form a red precipitate of $\mathrm{Cu}_{2}\mathrm{O}$.

Aromatic aldehydes like $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CHO}$ do not reduce Fehling's solution.

$\alpha$-hydroxy ketones like $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$ also reduce Fehling's solution because they tautomerize into an enediol intermediate in the alkaline medium, which then gets oxidized. Thus, both $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CHO}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$ give a positive Fehling's test and cannot be distinguished by it.

Since $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CHO}$ gives a positive test and $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CHO}$ gives a negative test, Fehling's solution can be used to distinguish between them.

Answer: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CHO}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CHO}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reagents can be used to carry out the transformation outlined below?
options: $\mathrm{BrMgCH}_{2}\mathrm{COOC}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}$
$\mathrm{BrZnCH}_{2}\mathrm{COOC}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}$
Any of these
$\mathrm{LiCH}_{2}\mathrm{COOC}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}$
questions.solution: Option text for (b) garbled in source OCR; answer key indicates (b).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following substances is NOT derived from formaldehyde?
options: Trioxane
Paraformaldehyde
Paraldehyde
Formalin
questions.solution:
Trioxane is a cyclic trimer of formaldehyde.

Paraformaldehyde is a linear polymer of formaldehyde.

Formalin is an aqueous solution of formaldehyde (approximately $40\%$ by volume).

Paraldehyde is a cyclic trimer of acetaldehyde, not formaldehyde.

Therefore, paraldehyde is not derived from formaldehyde.

Answer: Paraldehyde

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Indane-1,2,3-trione (the anhydrous form of ninhydrin) contains three keto groups, as shown in the structure below. Which of the labelled keto groups is expected to undergo hydration most easily?
options: All are equally hydrated
questions.solution:
In indane-1,2,3-trione, there are three adjacent keto groups. The reactivity of a carbonyl group towards nucleophilic addition (like hydration) depends on the magnitude of the positive charge (electrophilicity) on the carbonyl carbon.

The carbonyl carbon at position 2 is flanked by two highly electron-withdrawing carbonyl groups at positions 1 and 3. Due to the strong $-I$ (inductive) effect of these adjacent carbonyl groups, the carbon at position 2 becomes highly electron-deficient and thus highly electrophilic.

On the other hand, the carbonyl groups at positions 1 and 3 are conjugated with the benzene ring. The $+R$ (resonance) effect of the benzene ring donates electron density to these carbonyl carbons, reducing their electrophilicity.

Furthermore, hydration at the central carbonyl group (position 2) relieves the strong electrostatic repulsion between the adjacent partial negative charges on the oxygen atoms of the three carbonyl groups. The resulting gem-diol is also stabilized by intramolecular hydrogen bonding with the adjacent carbonyl oxygens at positions 1 and 3.

Therefore, the keto group at position 2 undergoes hydration most easily, forming ninhydrin.

Answer: 2

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 2-furylglyoxal, an \alpha-ketoaldehyde.

Treatment with concentrated base followed by acidic workup results in an intramolecular Cannizzaro reaction.

The hydroxide ion ($OH^{-}$) attacks the more electrophilic aldehyde carbonyl carbon to form a tetrahedral intermediate.

A 1,2-hydride shift occurs from the aldehyde carbon to the adjacent ketone carbonyl carbon.

Rapid intramolecular proton transfer then forms a stable carboxylate ion.

$R-CO-CHO + OH^{-} \rightarrow R-CO-CH(OH)-O^{-}$

$R-CO-CH(OH)-O^{-} \rightarrow R-C(O^{-})(H)-COOH$

$R-C(O^{-})(H)-COOH \rightarrow R-CH(OH)-COO^{-}$

Acidic workup with $H_3O^{+}$ protonates the carboxylate to yield the final \alpha-hydroxy acid.

$R-CH(OH)-COO^{-} + H_3O^{+} \rightarrow R-CH(OH)-COOH$

Substituting the 2-furyl group for $R$ gives 2-furylglycolic acid, which has the $-CH(OH)COOH$ group attached at the 2-position of the furan ring.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following intermediates is not formed during the iodoform reaction of acetone?
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCHI}_{2}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{2}\mathrm{I}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCI}_{3}$
$\mathrm{ICH}_{2}\mathrm{COCH}_{2}\mathrm{I}$
questions.solution:
The iodoform reaction of acetone involves the base-catalyzed halogenation of the alpha-carbon.

The introduction of an iodine atom increases the acidity of the remaining alpha-hydrogens on the same carbon atom due to its electron-withdrawing inductive effect.

As a result, successive halogenations occur more rapidly at the already halogenated carbon rather than at the unhalogenated methyl group.

The sequence of intermediates formed is $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{2}\mathrm{I}$, followed by $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCHI}_{2}$, and then $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCI}_{3}$.

Halogenation does not occur on both alpha-carbons simultaneously under these conditions. Therefore, $\mathrm{ICH}_{2}\mathrm{COCH}_{2}\mathrm{I}$ is not formed during the reaction.

Answer: $\mathrm{ICH}_{2}\mathrm{COCH}_{2}\mathrm{I}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following methods is most suitable for the reduction of a carbonyl compound to a hydrocarbon if the compound is sensitive to both strong acids and strong bases?
options: Thioacetal reduction
All of these
Wolff-Kishner reduction
Clemmensen reduction
questions.solution:
Clemmensen reduction involves the use of zinc amalgam and concentrated hydrochloric acid, which provides a strongly acidic medium. It is not suitable for acid-sensitive compounds.

Wolff-Kishner reduction involves the use of hydrazine and a strong base like potassium hydroxide or sodium hydroxide, which provides a strongly basic medium. It is not suitable for base-sensitive compounds.

Thioacetal reduction (Mozingo reduction) involves the conversion of the carbonyl compound to a thioacetal using a dithiol in the presence of a mild Lewis acid catalyst, followed by desulfurization using Raney nickel. This method avoids both strong aqueous acids and strong bases, making it the most suitable choice for compounds sensitive to both.

Answer: Thioacetal reduction

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following two structures:

(i)

(ii)

Which of the following statements correctly compares the electrophilic character of the carbonyl carbons in these structures?
options: The relative electrophilicity depends upon the complete structure of the compound.
The carbonyl carbons of both structures have equal electrophilic character.
The carbonyl carbon of (i) is more electrophilic than that of (ii).
The carbonyl carbon of (i) is less electrophilic than that of (ii).
questions.solution:
The electrophilic character of a carbonyl carbon depends on the magnitude of the partial positive charge it carries.

In structure (i), the neutral oxygen atom is more electronegative than carbon and withdraws electron density, creating a partial positive charge ($\delta^+$) on the carbonyl carbon.

In structure (ii), the oxygen atom is protonated and carries a formal positive charge. A positively charged oxygen atom is significantly more electronegative than a neutral oxygen atom. Consequently, it exerts a much stronger electron-withdrawing inductive effect ($-I$ effect) and resonance effect, pulling electron density away from the carbon atom more effectively.

This results in a larger positive charge on the carbonyl carbon in structure (ii) compared to structure (i). Therefore, the carbonyl carbon in the protonated structure (ii) is more electrophilic than the neutral carbonyl carbon in structure (i).

Thus, the carbonyl carbon of (i) is less electrophilic than that of (ii).

Answer: The carbonyl carbon of (i) is less electrophilic than that of (ii).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the major organic product formed when ethyl methyl ketone is treated with peracetic acid?
options: Both ethyl acetate and methyl propanoate
Only acetic acid
Ethyl acetate
Methyl propanoate
questions.solution:
The reaction of a ketone with a peroxy acid (such as peracetic acid) to form an ester is known as the Baeyer-Villiger oxidation.

In this reaction, an oxygen atom is inserted between the carbonyl carbon and one of the adjacent alkyl groups. The regioselectivity of the insertion is determined by the migratory aptitude of the alkyl groups, which follows the order: $3^{\circ} > 2^{\circ} > 1^{\circ} > \text{methyl}$.

The given substrate is ethyl methyl ketone ($CH_3-CO-CH_2CH_3$). The two groups attached to the carbonyl carbon are an ethyl group ($1^{\circ}$ alkyl) and a methyl group. Since the ethyl group has a higher migratory aptitude than the methyl group, it preferentially migrates to the oxygen atom.

As a result, the oxygen atom is inserted between the carbonyl carbon and the ethyl group, yielding ethyl acetate ($CH_3COOCH_2CH_3$) as the major organic product.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following reaction:


Which of the following reagents can be used as $\mathrm{R}$ to successfully carry out this transformation?

I. $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{MgBr}$
II. $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{Li}$
III. $(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2})_{2}\mathrm{Cd}$
IV. $(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2})_{2}\mathrm{CuLi}$
options: I, II or III
I or II
Any of the four
III or IV
questions.solution:
The given transformation involves the conversion of an acid chloride ($p$-nitrobenzoyl chloride) to a ketone ($p$-nitropropiophenone).

Grignard reagents (like $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{MgBr}$) and organolithium reagents (like $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{Li}$) are highly reactive nucleophiles. They react with acid chlorides to initially form ketones, but they rapidly react further with the resulting ketones to yield tertiary alcohols. Thus, reagents I and II cannot be used to stop the reaction at the ketone stage.

On the other hand, dialkylcadmium reagents (like $(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2})_{2}\mathrm{Cd}$) and lithium dialkylcuprates or Gilman reagents (like $(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2})_{2}\mathrm{CuLi}$) are milder, less reactive organometallic reagents. They selectively react with acid chlorides to yield ketones and do not undergo nucleophilic addition to the formed ketones.

Therefore, both $(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2})_{2}\mathrm{Cd}$ (III) and $(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2})_{2}\mathrm{CuLi}$ (IV) can successfully carry out this transformation.

Answer: III or IV

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is butanal, which is an aldehyde with a four-carbon chain ($\text{CH}_3\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-CHO}$).

The reagent $\text{NaBH}_4$ (sodium borohydride) is a mild reducing agent that specifically reduces aldehydes and ketones to their corresponding alcohols. It does not reduce carboxylic acids, esters, or amides.

When butanal is treated with $\text{NaBH}_4$ followed by acidic hydrolysis ($\text{H}^+, \text{H}_2\text{O}$), the carbonyl group ($\text{C=O}$) of the aldehyde is reduced to a primary alcohol ($\text{-CH}_2\text{OH}$).

The reaction is as follows:
$\text{CH}_3\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-CHO} \xrightarrow{\text{(i) NaBH}_4, \text{(ii) H}^+, \text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{CH}_3\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-OH}$

The product formed is butan-1-ol. Looking at the given options:
- Option (A) represents a secondary alcohol (pentan-2-ol).
- Option (B) represents butan-1-ol, where the terminal carbon is bonded to an $\text{-OH}$ group and two hydrogen atoms (explicitly shown with wedge and dash bonds).
- Option (C) represents pentan-2-ol.
- Option (D) represents butanoic acid, which would be an oxidation product.

Therefore, the correct major product is butan-1-ol, which corresponds to Option (B).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reactions leads to the formation of a product containing a carbon-carbon double bond (assuming the carbonyl compounds possess at least one $\alpha$-hydrogen)?
options: $2^{\circ}$ amine $+$ $\mathrm{R}_{2}\mathrm{CO} \longrightarrow$
Both $2^{\circ}$ amine $+$ $\mathrm{R}_{2}\mathrm{CO}$ and $2^{\circ}$ amine $+$ $\mathrm{RCHO}$
$1^{\circ}$ amine $+$ $\mathrm{RCHO} \longrightarrow$
$2^{\circ}$ amine $+$ $\mathrm{RCHO} \longrightarrow$
questions.solution:
The reaction of a primary amine with an aldehyde or ketone yields an imine, which contains a carbon-nitrogen double bond ($\mathrm{C=N}$).

The reaction of a secondary amine with an aldehyde or ketone possessing at least one $\alpha$-hydrogen yields an enamine. The mechanism involves the initial formation of a carbinolamine, which undergoes dehydration. Since there is no proton on the nitrogen atom to be lost, a proton is removed from the $\alpha$-carbon, resulting in the formation of a carbon-carbon double bond ($\mathrm{C=C}$).

Therefore, both $2^{\circ}$ amine $+$ $\mathrm{R}_{2}\mathrm{CO}$ and $2^{\circ}$ amine $+$ $\mathrm{RCHO}$ lead to the formation of an enamine, which contains a carbon-carbon double bond.

Answer: Both $2^{\circ}$ amine $+$ $\mathrm{R}_{2}\mathrm{CO}$ and $2^{\circ}$ amine $+$ $\mathrm{RCHO}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following statements are true regarding the general preparation of aldehydes and ketones from alcohols?

(i) Both can be prepared by the oxidation (dehydrogenation) of the corresponding alcohol using heated copper at about $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C} - 300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.
(ii) Both can be prepared by the Oppenauer oxidation of the corresponding alcohol.
(iii) Both can be prepared by the oxidation of the corresponding alcohol with aqueous acidic dichromate.
options: Only (i)
Only (i) and (iii)
Only (ii) and (iii)
(i), (ii), and (iii)
questions.solution:
Statement (i): When vapours of primary and secondary alcohols are passed over heated copper at $573\mathrm{K}$ ($300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$), dehydrogenation takes place. Primary alcohols form aldehydes and secondary alcohols form ketones. Thus, this is a general method for preparing both.

Statement (ii): Oppenauer oxidation is a specific method used primarily for the oxidation of secondary alcohols to ketones. It is not a general method for preparing aldehydes from primary alcohols, as aldehydes tend to undergo aldol condensation under the basic conditions of the reaction.

Statement (iii): Oxidation of primary alcohols with aqueous acidic dichromate generally leads to over-oxidation, yielding carboxylic acids rather than stopping at the aldehyde stage. To prepare aldehydes, anhydrous reagents like PCC or PDC are required. Therefore, aqueous acidic dichromate is not a general method for preparing aldehydes.

Thus, only statement (i) is true.

Answer: Only (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the appropriate reagent(s) for the following transformation:
options: $\mathrm{H_2NCH_2CH_3}$, $\mathrm{CH_3CO_2H}$, removal of water
$\mathrm{H_2NCH_3}$, $\mathrm{H_2O}$, removal of water
$\mathrm{H_2NCH_2CH_2CH_3}$, $\mathrm{CH_3CO_2H}$, removal of water
$\mathrm{H_2N-Ph}$, removal of water
questions.solution:
The given transformation involves the conversion of a ketone (cyclopentanone) into an imine (a Schiff base).

The formation of an imine from a ketone requires a primary amine. The general reaction is:
$\mathrm{R_2C=O} + \mathrm{R'-NH_2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H^+}} \mathrm{R_2C=N-R'} + \mathrm{H_2O}$

By observing the structure of the product, the nitrogen atom is attached to an ethyl group ($-\mathrm{CH_2CH_3}$). Therefore, the primary amine required for this reaction is ethylamine ($\mathrm{H_2NCH_2CH_3}$).

This condensation reaction is typically catalyzed by a mild acid, such as acetic acid ($\mathrm{CH_3CO_2H}$), which protonates the carbonyl oxygen to make it more electrophilic. To drive the equilibrium towards the formation of the imine, the water produced during the reaction must be continuously removed.

Comparing this with the given options, the correct set of reagents and conditions is $\mathrm{H_2NCH_2CH_3}$, $\mathrm{CH_3CO_2H}$, and the removal of water.

Answer: $\mathrm{H_2NCH_2CH_3}$, $\mathrm{CH_3CO_2H}$, removal of water

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the given reaction.
questions.solution:
The given reactant is pentan-2-one, which is a ketone.

The reaction involves two steps:
1. Nucleophilic addition: The Grignard reagent, methylmagnesium bromide ($\text{CH}_3\text{MgBr}$), provides a methyl nucleophile ($\text{CH}_3^-$). This nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of pentan-2-one. The $\pi$ bond of the carbonyl group breaks, transferring electrons to the oxygen atom to form an alkoxide intermediate.
$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{C}(=\text{O})\text{CH}_3 + \text{CH}_3\text{MgBr} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{C}(\text{O}^-\text{MgBr}^+)(\text{CH}_3)_2$

2. Acidic workup: The addition of dilute acid ($\text{H}^+, \text{H}_2\text{O}$) protonates the alkoxide intermediate to yield the final alcohol product.
$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{C}(\text{O}^-\text{MgBr}^+)(\text{CH}_3)_2 + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{C}(\text{OH})(\text{CH}_3)_2$

The resulting product is a tertiary alcohol, specifically 2-methylpentan-2-ol.

Looking at the given options, Option (D) correctly represents the structure of 2-methylpentan-2-ol, showing the central carbon bonded to a propyl group, a hydroxyl group, and two methyl groups (indicated by the wedge and dash bonds to illustrate tetrahedral geometry).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 5-oxohexanoic acid, which contains both a ketone and a carboxylic acid functional group.

Step 1: Reaction with $\text{NaBH}_4$
$\text{NaBH}_4$ is a mild and selective reducing agent. It reduces aldehydes and ketones to their corresponding alcohols but does not reduce carboxylic acids. Therefore, the ketone group at C-5 is reduced to a secondary alcohol, while the carboxylic acid group at C-1 remains unaffected. The intermediate formed is 5-hydroxyhexanoic acid.
$\text{CH}_3-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{COOH} \xrightarrow{\text{NaBH}_4} \text{CH}_3-\text{CH}(\text{OH})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{COOH}$

Step 2: Reaction with $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$
In the presence of an acidic medium, the intermediate 5-hydroxyhexanoic acid undergoes intramolecular esterification (lactonization). The hydroxyl group at C-5 acts as a nucleophile and attacks the carbonyl carbon of the carboxylic acid group at C-1. This results in the elimination of a water molecule and the formation of a stable 6-membered cyclic ester ($\delta$-lactone).

The resulting ring contains five carbon atoms and one oxygen atom. The oxygen is bonded to the carbonyl carbon on one side and to the carbon bearing the methyl group on the other side. This structure corresponds to 6-methyltetrahydro-2H-pyran-2-one, which is shown in option (D).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is least reactive towards nucleophilic addition?
options: Propanone
3-Pentanone
Methanal
2-Pentanone
questions.solution:
The reactivity of carbonyl compounds towards nucleophilic addition depends on steric and electronic factors. Reactivity decreases as the size and number of alkyl groups attached to the carbonyl carbon increase, due to greater steric hindrance and increased +I (electron-donating) effect, which reduces the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon.

Methanal ($HCHO$) has no alkyl groups and is the most reactive.

Propanone ($CH_3COCH_3$) has two small methyl groups.

2-Pentanone ($CH_3COCH_2CH_2CH_3$) has one methyl and one propyl group. The presence of the small methyl group leaves one side of the carbonyl carbon relatively open for nucleophilic attack.

3-Pentanone ($CH_3CH_2COCH_2CH_3$) has two ethyl groups. Both sides of the carbonyl carbon are hindered by these bulkier groups, making the approach of a nucleophile the most difficult among the given options.

Therefore, 3-pentanone is the least reactive towards nucleophilic addition.

Answer: 3-Pentanone

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction involves the acid-catalyzed rearrangement of a spiro ketone.

First, the carbonyl oxygen of the spiro[3.5]nonan-5-one is protonated by concentrated $H_2SO_4$, generating a resonance-stabilized carbocation at the carbonyl carbon.

Next, to relieve the significant angle strain of the four-membered cyclobutane ring, a C-C bond from this ring migrates to the adjacent carbocation center. This ring expansion results in a fused bicyclic intermediate containing a five-membered and a six-membered ring, with a tertiary carbocation at one bridgehead position and a hydroxyl group at the other.

To further stabilize the system and form a stable oxonium ion, a second rearrangement occurs. A C-C bond from the six-membered ring migrates to the carbocation center. This ring contraction converts the six-membered ring into a five-membered ring and shifts the positive charge to the carbon bonded to the hydroxyl group, forming a highly stable protonated ketone.

Finally, deprotonation of this intermediate yields spiro[4.4]nonan-1-one as the major product.

This final structure consists of two spiro-fused five-membered rings with a ketone group adjacent to the spiro carbon, which corresponds to the first option.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the appropriate reagent for the following transformation.
options: $\mathrm{HOCH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$
questions.solution:
The given transformation is the conversion of a ketone (acetophenone) into a ketal.

The product is a diethyl ketal, as it contains two ethoxy ($-\mathrm{OCH_2CH_3}$) groups attached to the same carbon atom.

Ketal formation occurs when a ketone reacts with two equivalents of a monohydric alcohol in the presence of an acid catalyst. The general reaction is:

$\mathrm{R_2C=O} + 2\mathrm{R'OH} \rightarrow \mathrm{R_2C(OR')_2} + \mathrm{H_2O}$

Since the product has ethoxy groups, the required alcohol is ethanol ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH}$).

The specific reaction is:

$\mathrm{C_6H_5COCH_3} + 2\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH} \rightarrow \mathrm{C_6H_5C(OCH_2CH_3)_2CH_3} + \mathrm{H_2O}$

Continuous removal of water shifts the equilibrium to the right, favoring ketal formation. Therefore, the appropriate reagent is $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH}$.

Answer: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major organic product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is acetophenone ($C_6H_5COCH_3$), which contains a methyl ketone group ($-COCH_3$).

When a methyl ketone is treated with excess halogen ($Cl_2$) in the presence of a base ($HO^-$), it undergoes the haloform reaction.

The reaction proceeds via the formation of a trichloromethyl intermediate, followed by cleavage of the $C-C$ bond by the hydroxide ion. This yields chloroform ($CHCl_3$) and the carboxylate ion corresponding to the rest of the molecule.

The balanced chemical equation is:
$C_6H_5COCH_3 + 3Cl_2 + 4OH^- \rightarrow C_6H_5COO^- + CHCl_3 + 3Cl^- + 3H_2O$

Since the reaction medium is basic and no subsequent acidification step is mentioned, the major organic product remains as the benzoate ion ($C_6H_5COO^-$).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An aldehydic group can be protected:
options: by both of the above methods.
by acetal formation against alkaline oxidizing agents.
by mercaptal formation against acidic oxidizing agents.
by neither of the above methods.
questions.solution:
Acetals are stable in neutral and alkaline media but undergo hydrolysis in acidic media. Thus, an aldehydic group can be protected by acetal formation against alkaline oxidizing agents.

Mercaptals (thioacetals) are stable in acidic media but are highly susceptible to oxidation. The sulfur atoms in mercaptals can be easily oxidized to sulfoxides or sulfones by acidic oxidizing agents. Therefore, they cannot be used to protect an aldehydic group against acidic oxidizing agents.

Hence, only acetal formation is a valid protection method against alkaline oxidizing agents.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:



(i) $\mathrm{HCN}$
(ii) $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$
(iii) $\mathrm{NaNO}_{2}, \mathrm{HCl}$
questions.solution:
The reaction of 1-indanone with $\mathrm{HCN}$ undergoes nucleophilic addition to form a cyanohydrin, 1-hydroxy-1-cyanoindane.

Subsequent reduction with $\mathrm{LiAlH_4}$ converts the cyano group ($-\mathrm{CN}$) into a primary amine ($-\mathrm{CH_2NH_2}$), producing 1-(aminomethyl)indan-1-ol.

Reaction with $\mathrm{NaNO_2}$ and $\mathrm{HCl}$ leads to diazotization of the primary amine. The aliphatic diazonium ion readily loses $\mathrm{N_2}$ to generate a primary carbocation at the exocyclic carbon ($-\mathrm{CH_2^+}$).

This species undergoes a Tiffeneau-Demjanov ring expansion. Due to the higher migratory aptitude of the aryl group compared to the alkyl group, the aryl bond (the bond between the hydroxyl-bearing carbon and the benzene ring) migrates to the exocyclic carbocation. This expands the five-membered ring into a six-membered ring.

The positive charge is transferred to the carbon bonded to the hydroxyl group, which then loses a proton to form a carbon-oxygen double bond. Since the aryl group migrated, the resulting ketone is separated from the benzene ring by one methylene group, yielding 2-tetralone.

This matches the structure in the fourth option.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction:

$\xrightarrow[\text{(ii) } \mathrm{H_2O}/\mathrm{H^+}]{\text{(i) Excess } \mathrm{MeMgCl}}$ Major Product
questions.solution:
The Grignard reagent ($MeMgCl$) acts as a strong base and first abstracts the acidic proton from the phenolic $-OH$ group to form a phenoxide ion.

Another equivalent of $MeMgCl$ acts as a nucleophile and attacks the carbonyl carbon to form a magnesium alkoxide intermediate.

The resulting $\alpha$-chloroalkoxide undergoes an intramolecular $S_{N}2$ reaction. The alkoxide oxygen displaces the chloride ion on the adjacent carbon, forming an epoxide ring.

Since $MeMgCl$ is present in excess, an additional equivalent attacks the less sterically hindered carbon atom ($CH_2$) of the newly formed epoxide ring. The epoxide undergoes ring opening to form a tertiary alkoxide with an ethyl chain.

Subsequent acidic workup ($H_2O/H^+$) protonates the phenoxide and alkoxide ions, yielding 2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)butan-2-ol as the final product.

The structure of this product corresponds to option (4).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence:
options: Product (b)
Product (c)
Product (a)
Product (d)
questions.solution:
The first step involves a 1,4-conjugate addition of the cyanide ion from $\text{NaCN}$ to the $\alpha,\beta$-unsaturated aldehyde, forming a $\beta$-cyano aldehyde.

$\text{Ph}-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CHO} \xrightarrow{\text{NaCN}} \text{Ph}-\text{CH}(\text{CN})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHO}$

In the second step, the aldehyde reacts with ammonia under mildly acidic conditions to form an imine.

$\text{Ph}-\text{CH}(\text{CN})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHO} \xrightarrow{\text{NH}_3 / \Delta / \text{H}^+} \text{Ph}-\text{CH}(\text{CN})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}=\text{NH}$

The imine then undergoes nucleophilic addition of $\text{HCN}$ to form an $\alpha$-amino nitrile.

$\text{Ph}-\text{CH}(\text{CN})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}=\text{NH} \xrightarrow{\text{HCN}} \text{Ph}-\text{CH}(\text{CN})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{NH}_2)-\text{CN}$

Finally, complete acidic hydrolysis converts both nitrile groups into carboxylic acids.

$\text{Ph}-\text{CH}(\text{CN})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{NH}_2)-\text{CN} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_3\text{O}^+} \text{HOOC}-\text{CH}(\text{Ph})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{NH}_2)-\text{COOH}$

The final product matches the structure of product (b).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the structure of the major product $\mathrm{Y}$ formed in the following base-catalyzed cyclization reaction.
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 4-acetyl-4-methylcyclohexan-1-one.

In the presence of a mild base like $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$, an intramolecular aldol condensation takes place. The base abstracts an acidic $\alpha$-hydrogen to form an enolate. The most favorable enolate for cyclization is formed by the deprotonation of the methyl group of the acetyl moiety, yielding a $-\text{C}(=\text{O})\text{CH}_2^-$ carbanion.

This nucleophilic carbanion undergoes an intramolecular attack on the electrophilic carbonyl carbon at C-1 of the cyclohexane ring. This attack forms a new carbon-carbon bond, creating a bicyclo[2.2.2]octane ring system.

Upon cyclization, the carbonyl oxygen at C-1 becomes an alkoxide, which is subsequently protonated by water to form a hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$) at the bridgehead position. The other bridgehead position (C-4) retains the original methyl group.

The newly formed bridge consists of the $-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{CH}_2-$ group. Since the acetyl group was originally attached to C-4, the carbonyl carbon remains directly attached to the C-4 bridgehead (which bears the methyl group), and the methylene carbon is attached to the C-1 bridgehead (which bears the hydroxyl group).

Comparing this connectivity with the given options, Option (A) correctly shows the bicyclo[2.2.2]octane skeleton with the $-\text{OH}$ at the bottom bridgehead, the $-\text{CH}_3$ at the top bridgehead, and the ketone carbonyl adjacent to the methyl-substituted bridgehead.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is an enol lactone, specifically a 4-oxa-steroid derivative with a double bond between C5 and C6.

Step 1: The Wittig reagent, methylenetriphenylphosphorane ($H_2C=P(Ph)_3$), attacks the carbonyl carbon (C3) of the enol lactone.

Step 2: Instead of undergoing the standard elimination to form an enol ether, the tetrahedral intermediate undergoes ring opening. The enolate leaving group is relatively stable, leading to the formation of a ring-opened intermediate containing a ketone at C5 and a phosphonium salt at C3: $-C(=O)-CH_2-P^{+}Ph_3$.

Step 3: The addition of a strong base ($RLi$) deprotonates the acidic methylene group adjacent to the phosphonium ion, generating a stabilized ylide: $-C(=O)-CH=PPh_3$.

Step 4: Upon heating ($\Delta$), an intramolecular Wittig reaction occurs between the newly formed ylide at C4 and the ketone at C5.

Step 5: This intramolecular olefination closes the ring, forming a new carbon-carbon double bond between C4 and C5, and leaving a ketone at C3. The resulting product is a standard $\Delta^4$-3-ketosteroid.

This structure matches the one given in Option (A).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 2,6-dimethylcyclohexane-1,4-dione, which contains two ketone groups.

The carbonyl group at the top position is sterically hindered by the two adjacent methyl groups. The carbonyl group at the bottom position is less sterically hindered as it is flanked by two $-\text{CH}_2-$ groups.

In step (i), the reaction with ethylene glycol ($\text{HO-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-OH}$) is sensitive to steric hindrance. It selectively protects the less hindered bottom carbonyl group by forming a cyclic ketal (acetal).

In step (ii), the Wittig reagent, methylenetriphenylphosphorane ($\text{PPh}_3=\text{CH}_2$), reacts with the remaining unprotected, sterically hindered top carbonyl group. This converts the $\text{C=O}$ group into a methylene group ($=\text{CH}_2$).

In step (iii), acidic hydrolysis ($\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$) cleaves the cyclic ketal, deprotecting it and regenerating the original bottom carbonyl group.

Therefore, the final major product has a methylene group ($=\text{CH}_2$) at the top position and a ketone group ($=\text{O}$) at the bottom position. This corresponds to the structure given in option (D).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 4,4-dimethylnaphthalen-1(4H)-one.

When treated with acetic anhydride ($Ac_2O$), it undergoes a dienone-phenol rearrangement.

The carbonyl oxygen is acylated by $Ac_2O$, forming an intermediate with a positive charge that delocalizes to the C3 carbon.

A 1,2-methyl shift takes place from the C4 position (gem-dimethyl group) to the C3 position, creating a more stable benzylic carbocation at C4.

Deprotonation at C3 restores the aromaticity of the ring, yielding a fully aromatic naphthalene system.

Since the reaction medium contains acetic anhydride, the oxygen is retained as an acetate ester ($-OAc$) rather than a free hydroxyl group ($-OH$).

The final product is 1-acetoxy-3,4-dimethylnaphthalene, which matches the structure in Option (A).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction.
questions.solution:
The given reaction is a Robinson annulation, which involves a Michael addition followed by an intramolecular aldol condensation.

First, the base ($NaOMe$) abstracts the acidic $\alpha$-proton from isobutyraldehyde to form an enolate ion:
$(CH_3)_2CH-CHO \xrightarrow{NaOMe} (CH_3)_2C^{-}-CHO + MeOH$

This enolate acts as a nucleophile and undergoes Michael addition with the $\alpha,\beta$-unsaturated ketone, methyl vinyl ketone (MVK):
$(CH_3)_2C^{-}-CHO + CH_2=CH-CO-CH_3 \rightarrow OHC-C(CH_3)_2-CH_2-CH_2-CO-CH_3$
The resulting intermediate is a 1,5-dicarbonyl compound, 2,2-dimethyl-5-oxohexanal.

Next, an intramolecular aldol condensation occurs. The base abstracts a proton from the terminal methyl group of the ketone to form a new enolate, which then attacks the aldehyde carbonyl carbon to form a stable six-membered ring:
$OHC-C(CH_3)_2-CH_2-CH_2-CO-CH_3 \xrightarrow{NaOMe} OHC-C(CH_3)_2-CH_2-CH_2-CO-CH_2^{-}$
Cyclization of this enolate yields the $\beta$-hydroxy ketone intermediate, 3-hydroxy-4,4-dimethylcyclohexan-1-one.

Finally, upon heating ($\Delta$), dehydration takes place. The hydroxyl group is at C-3. Since C-4 is a quaternary carbon with no hydrogen atoms, the elimination of water must occur between C-2 and C-3. This forms a double bond conjugated with the ketone:
3-hydroxy-4,4-dimethylcyclohexan-1-one $\xrightarrow{\Delta}$ 4,4-dimethylcyclohex-2-en-1-one + $H_2O$.

The structure of 4,4-dimethylcyclohex-2-en-1-one matches the compound shown in option (B).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the acid-catalyzed condensation (cyclotrimerization) of butan-2-one (ethyl methyl ketone) in the presence of concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$.

In acid-catalyzed aldol-type reactions, enolization preferentially occurs at the more substituted $\alpha$-carbon to form the more stable (thermodynamic) enol. For butan-2-one, the enolization occurs at the methylene ($\text{-CH}_2\text{-}$) group rather than the methyl ($\text{-CH}_3$) group.

Three molecules of butan-2-one undergo condensation where the carbonyl carbon of one molecule reacts with the nucleophilic methylene carbon of another molecule. This sequence continues to form a six-membered ring.

Let's trace the atoms:
The structure of butan-2-one is $\text{CH}_3\text{-C(=O)-CH}_2\text{-CH}_3$.
The ring is formed by the alternating connection of the carbonyl carbons and the methylene carbons from the three molecules.
Since the carbonyl carbon is attached to a methyl group and the methylene carbon is also attached to a methyl group, every carbon atom that makes up the newly formed benzene ring will have a methyl group attached to it.

The overall reaction can be written as:
$3 \text{ CH}_3\text{-CO-CH}_2\text{-CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{Conc. H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{C}_6(\text{CH}_3)_6 + 3 \text{ H}_2\text{O}$

The major product is 1,2,3,4,5,6-hexamethylbenzene, which has a methyl group on all six positions of the benzene ring. This matches the structure given in option (D).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given reactant contains two carbonyl groups: an ester ($-\text{COOMe}$) and an aldehyde ($-\text{CHO}$).

Step 1: The aldehyde group is more reactive towards nucleophilic addition than the ester group. Therefore, reaction with ethylene glycol ($\text{HO}-(\text{CH}_2)_2-\text{OH}$) in the presence of an acid catalyst ($\text{H}^+$) selectively protects the aldehyde group by forming a cyclic acetal. The ester group remains unaffected.
Intermediate formed: $\text{MeO}-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{OCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{O})$

Step 2: The protected intermediate is then treated with excess methylmagnesium bromide ($\text{MeMgBr}$). The Grignard reagent reacts with the ester group. Two equivalents of $\text{MeMgBr}$ add to the ester to form a tertiary alkoxide.
Intermediate formed: $\text{BrMg}-\text{O}-\text{C}(\text{Me})_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{OCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{O})$

Step 3: Finally, acidic workup ($\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$) serves two purposes:
1. It protonates the tertiary alkoxide to yield a tertiary alcohol.
2. It hydrolyzes the cyclic acetal back to the original aldehyde group.

Final product: $\text{HO}-\text{C}(\text{Me})_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHO}$ (3-hydroxy-3-methylbutanal).

This structure corresponds to option (B).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given starting material is ethyl 4-oxobutanoate, which contains both an aldehyde group and an ester group.

Step (i): Reaction with ethylene glycol ($\text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$) in the presence of an acid catalyst ($\text{H}^+$) and heat. Aldehydes are more reactive towards nucleophilic addition than esters. Therefore, the aldehyde group is selectively protected as a cyclic acetal (1,3-dioxolane), while the ester group remains unreacted.

Step (ii): Reaction with lithium aluminum hydride ($\text{LiAlH}_4$) followed by mild acid workup. $\text{LiAlH}_4$ is a strong reducing agent that reduces the ester group to a primary alcohol ($-\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$). The cyclic acetal is stable under these basic/hydride reducing conditions and remains intact.

Step (iii): Reaction with aqueous acid ($\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$) and heat. This acidic hydrolysis step deprotects the cyclic acetal, regenerating the original aldehyde group.

The final product is 4-hydroxybutanal, which has the structure $\text{HO}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHO}$. This matches the structure given in option (D).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence:



(i) $\mathrm{Br}_{2}, \mathrm{NaOH}$
(ii) $\mathrm{MeOH} / \mathrm{H}^{+}$
(iii) $\mathrm{NaOMe}$
questions.solution:
The given starting material is 3-(2-acetylphenyl)propanoic acid.

Step (i): The reagent $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{NaOH}$ is used for the haloform reaction. The methyl ketone group ($-\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$) is oxidized to a carboxylate salt, while the propanoic acid group is also converted to its sodium salt. Upon acidic workup (implied for the next step), the product is a dicarboxylic acid, 2-(2-carboxyethyl)benzoic acid.

Step (ii): Treatment with $\mathrm{MeOH}$ and $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ leads to Fischer esterification of both carboxylic acid groups. This yields a diester, methyl 2-(3-methoxy-3-oxopropyl)benzoate.

Step (iii): The addition of the base $\mathrm{NaOMe}$ initiates a Dieckmann condensation (intramolecular Claisen condensation). The base abstracts an $\alpha$-proton from the propanoate ester group to form an enolate. This nucleophilic enolate attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of the benzoate ester group. The intramolecular attack forms a stable 5-membered ring fused to the benzene ring. Subsequent loss of a methoxide ion yields the cyclic $\beta$-keto ester.

The final product is 2-methoxycarbonyl-1-indanone, which matches the structure in option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The reaction proceeds via a tandem oxa-Michael addition followed by an intramolecular aldol condensation.

1. Oxa-Michael Addition: In the presence of a base ($OH^-$), the phenolic -OH group of salicylaldehyde is deprotonated to form a phenoxide ion. This nucleophilic phenoxide ion attacks the $\beta$-carbon of the $\alpha,\beta$-unsaturated ketone (methyl vinyl ketone) in a conjugate addition manner. This forms the intermediate 2-(3-oxobutoxy)benzaldehyde.

2. Intramolecular Aldol Condensation: The intermediate contains both an aldehyde and a ketone. The base abstracts an $\alpha$-proton from the $CH_2$ group of the ketone to form an enolate. This specific enolate is favored because its subsequent nucleophilic attack on the electrophilic aldehyde carbon leads to the formation of a stable 6-membered ring (a chromanol derivative).

3. Dehydration: The resulting cyclic aldol adduct has a hydroxyl group at the C4 position and a proton at the C3 position. Upon heating ($\Delta$), dehydration occurs via the elimination of $H_2O$ from C3 and C4. This forms a double bond between C3 and C4.

The resulting product is 3-acetyl-2H-chromene. The newly formed double bond is highly stable as it is in extended conjugation with both the adjacent benzene ring and the carbonyl group of the acetyl substituent. This structure corresponds to Option (A).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the reactant that yields the given product through the following sequence of reactions:

(i) $\mathrm{HO}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{OH}$, catalytic $\mathrm{H}^{+}$
(ii) $\mathrm{Mg}$, $\mathrm{THF}$; then $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}$; then $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{+}$
(iii) $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{+}$, heat

Product:
questions.solution:
The product is a cyclic hemiacetal containing a 6-membered ring fused to a benzene ring. The hemiacetal carbon is bonded to a methyl group, indicating it is derived from the intramolecular cyclization of a hydroxyl group with a methyl ketone.

Evaluating the reaction sequence for the reactant in Option C gives the desired product.

In the first step, the ketone group $\mathrm{-COCH_3}$ of 1-(2-(bromomethyl)phenyl)ethan-1-one is protected as a cyclic ketal using $\mathrm{HO-CH_2CH_2-OH}$ and catalytic $\mathrm{H^+}$. The $\mathrm{-CH_2Br}$ group remains unaffected.

In the second step, Mg in THF converts the $\mathrm{-CH_2Br}$ group into a Grignard reagent $\mathrm{-CH_2MgBr}$. This reacts with formaldehyde $\mathrm{H_2C=O}$ followed by mild acidic workup to form a primary alcohol, extending the chain to $\mathrm{-CH_2CH_2OH}$.

In the third step, heating with $\mathrm{H_3O^+}$ deprotects the ketal back to the methyl ketone, yielding the intermediate 1-(2-(2-hydroxyethyl)phenyl)ethan-1-one.

Under these acidic conditions, the primary hydroxyl group $\mathrm{-OH}$ attacks the carbonyl carbon of the methyl ketone. This intramolecular nucleophilic addition forms a stable 6-membered cyclic hemiacetal.

The resulting structure has a methyl group and a hydroxyl group on the carbon adjacent to the benzene ring, which perfectly matches the given product. Other options either lack a methyl ketone, yielding a hemiacetal with a hydrogen atom instead of a methyl group, or form a ring of a different size.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following reaction sequence:



Reagents:
(i) BuLi
(ii) $0.5$ equivalent $\mathrm{Br}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{Br}$
(iii) Dil. $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$
(iv) $\mathrm{OH}^- / \Delta$

Identify the major product formed.
questions.solution:
Step 1: The starting material is 2-ethyl-1,3-dithiane. Treatment with BuLi, a strong base, abstracts the acidic proton at the C2 position (between the two sulfur atoms), forming a dithiane carbanion.

Step 2: Reaction with $0.5$ equivalents of 1,2-dibromoethane ($\mathrm{Br}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{Br}$) leads to the alkylation of two dithiane anions. Each anion displaces a bromide ion, forming a bridged intermediate: 1,2-bis(2-ethyl-1,3-dithian-2-yl)ethane.

Step 3: Hydrolysis with dilute $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ deprotects the dithiane groups, converting them back to carbonyl groups. The C2 carbon of each dithiane ring becomes a carbonyl carbon. This yields the diketone octane-3,6-dione: $\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{C}(=\mathrm{O})-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{C}(=\mathrm{O})-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_3$.

Step 4: Under basic conditions ($\mathrm{OH}^-$) and heat ($\Delta$), the diketone undergoes an intramolecular aldol condensation. Deprotonation can occur at the $\alpha$-carbons (C2 or C4). Deprotonation at C4 followed by attack on the C6 carbonyl would form a highly strained 3-membered ring. Therefore, deprotonation occurs at C2, forming an enolate that attacks the C6 carbonyl to form a stable 5-membered ring. The intermediate formed is 2-methyl-3-ethyl-3-hydroxycyclopentan-1-one.

Step 5: Heating causes dehydration of this $\beta$-hydroxy ketone. Loss of water occurs to form the more stable, conjugated $\alpha,\beta$-unsaturated ketone. The double bond forms between C2 and C3, yielding 2-methyl-3-ethylcyclopent-2-en-1-one.

This final structure corresponds to the molecule shown in option (D).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence:



(i) $\mathrm{LDA}$
(ii) $\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{I}$
(iii) $\mathrm{NaBH}_4$ / $\mathrm{MeOH}$
questions.solution:
The given starting material is cyclopentanone.

Step (i): Reaction with $\mathrm{LDA}$ (Lithium diisopropylamide), which is a strong, bulky base. It abstracts an $\alpha$-proton from cyclopentanone to form a lithium enolate.

Step (ii): Reaction with $\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{I}$ (Methyl iodide). The enolate acts as a nucleophile and undergoes an $S_N2$ reaction with $\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{I}$, resulting in the $\alpha$-alkylation of the ketone to form 2-methylcyclopentanone.

Step (iii): Reaction with $\mathrm{NaBH}_4$ in $\mathrm{MeOH}$. Sodium borohydride is a reducing agent that reduces the ketone carbonyl group to a secondary alcohol. Thus, 2-methylcyclopentanone is reduced to 2-methylcyclopentanol.

Comparing this with the given options, the final major product is 2-methylcyclopentanol.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The reaction sequence begins with the hydration of the alkyne group in 2-methyl-2-(prop-1-ynyl)cyclohexan-1-one using $1\% \text{ HgSO}_4$ and dil. $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$. Due to significant steric hindrance from the adjacent quaternary carbon, the hydration regioselectively occurs at the less hindered carbon of the triple bond. This yields 2-methyl-2-(2-oxopropyl)cyclohexan-1-one, which is a 1,4-diketone intermediate.

In the second step, the base ($\text{OH}^-$) abstracts a proton from the terminal methyl group of the side chain to form an enolate. This enolate undergoes an intramolecular nucleophilic addition to the cyclohexanone carbonyl carbon, forming a fused 5-membered ring.

Subsequent heating ($\Delta$) causes dehydration of the resulting aldol. Under thermodynamic control, the elimination of water favors the formation of the more stable $\alpha,\beta$-unsaturated ketone. The double bond forms within the 5-membered ring such that it is conjugated with the newly formed carbonyl group. This corresponds to the major product shown in Option (D).

Answer:
